chapters.title: Gaining wisdom from struggles
chapters.text:
We often think of poverty as a lack of money or basic necessities, but it runs much deeper, seeping into the emotional and mental well-being of those it touches. It’s a burden that chips away at the spirit, erodes dreams, and traps generations in cycles that seem impossible to escape. In the story of Sarah Smarsh’s family, poverty isn’t just about missing paychecks or empty refrigerators — it’s about the constant, grinding battle to survive in rural Kansas, where hard work is abundant but the rewards are scarce.

Hope thrives when hard work becomes a path, not a burden.

Smarsh grew up surrounded by resilient women who carried the weight of their families’ struggles on their shoulders. Her grandmother Betty, a symbol of endurance, moved from one low-wage job to another, trying to outrun the poverty that followed her. Her mother, Jeannie, became a teenage mom, following in the footsteps of generations of women whose dreams were often deferred for survival. Smarsh’s father, Nick, a blue-collar worker whose foundation-laying business crumbled with the 1980s economic downturn, added to the family’s enduring struggle for financial stability.

This tale is not just of hardship but of resilience and determination. Smarsh escaped the cycle through education, but her story is rooted in the sacrifices of her family. Their experiences offer lessons in strength and survival against impossible odds.

Follow this summary to learn how their journey reflects the broader struggles of working-class America and discover how their story can guide us in understanding — and overcoming — the cycles of poverty.

chapters.title: The price of the American Dream
chapters.text:
The idea that anyone can succeed through hard work is a powerful part of America’s identity, but for the poor, it’s a myth that rarely delivers. Smarsh’s family, like many others, believed in hard work as the key to success, often using creative methods to generate income. From running a fireworks stand to working multiple jobs, they never stopped hustling. Yet, no matter how hard they worked, they could never climb out of poverty because the system wasn’t designed to help them succeed.

The American Dream promises success, but the cost for the poverty-stricken is often too high to pay.

Betty worked as a waitress, cleaning homes and taking any odd job she could find, believing in the value of hard work. Yet, the family seemed to move one step forward and two steps back with every effort. Smarsh experienced those harsh realities as a child — watching her family’s efforts fail to bear fruit despite their sacrifices and understanding early on that the American Dream was far out of reach for people like them.

Smarsh’s family constantly faced a choice: work themselves to exhaustion or fall even further behind. Smarsh recalls watching her father work construction jobs under the brutal Kansas sunrises and her mother enduring shifts in physically demanding jobs — an endless hustle that defined their lives but gave little in return.

The truth is that the less you have, the more you have to sacrifice to chase that elusive dream. Class divisions were invisible, but they created barriers that made it impossible for families like Smarsh’s to reach the promised lands of the American Dream. The notion of meritocracy collapses when the playing field is so uneven that those starting from the bottom must pay more and work harder yet still end up with nothing.

This constant struggle creates an emotional toll that accompanies the financial strain. It’s not just about having enough money; it’s about constantly feeling that success remains out of reach no matter how hard you work. This realization redefines the American Dream, exposing it as a system where wealth determines access, and for the poor, the cost is too high.

If you’re wild enough to enjoy it, poverty can contain a sort of freedom… ~ Sarah Smarsh

chapters.title: Poverty and motherhood
chapters.text:
Single mothers bear the brunt of poverty’s harshest blows. Smarsh’s mother and grandmother, like many other women in their community, faced a relentless struggle to provide for their families while enduring the emotional strain of poverty. Every day was a balancing act — finding ways to feed their children, keep them clothed, and somehow maintain a sense of normalcy in a world that felt constantly on the edge of collapse.

Motherhood, already challenging, became a battlefield where survival often took precedence over emotional care. Women like Jeannie, Smarsh’s mother, couldn’t afford to spend time nurturing because they were too focused on working long hours to ensure there was food on the table. Jeannie’s choice to work construction while raising Sarah showed the delicate balancing act between being both the provider and the emotional anchor for her family.

Emotional neglect was not a choice but a byproduct of survival, a sacrifice many single mothers made daily.

For the children, this meant growing up in a world where affection and attention were luxuries, not givens. Smarsh’s story underscores the invisible strain that poverty places on families, forcing mothers to make impossible choices between providing for their children’s basic needs and giving them the emotional support they desperately need. Yet, even in the toughest circumstances, these women displayed remarkable resilience, proving that love doesn’t always need to be expressed with time and attention — it can be found in their relentless drive to keep their families afloat.

Betty, who had little time for emotional nurturing with her own children, showed her love in practical ways — keeping them fed, clothed, and safe. Smarsh reflects on how this form of love, though less visible, shaped the emotional landscape of her family. It was a difficult balance between survival and care, with the physical demands of poverty leaving little space for emotional expression.

chapters.title: From Farm to Freedom
chapters.text:
Growing up on a Kansas farm, Smarsh lived a life filled with the beauty of nature and the harsh realities of rural poverty. The farm was both a blessing and a burden, offering a sense of place and belonging while also shackling her family to the uncertainties of the agricultural economy. The 1980s farm crisis devastated farming communities across the Midwest, including the one the Smarsh family called home. As land values plummeted and loans mounted, farmers who once enjoyed feeling ownership over their land found themselves losing their livelihoods. This economic collapse turned the farm from a sanctuary into a kind of prison, trapping families in cycles of debt and labor with little hope for financial stability.

If you are frustrated by limited opportunities, try finding hope in learning.

Smarsh recalls the isolation and vulnerability that came with rural life. Access to education, healthcare, and even basic necessities was limited, and the burden of maintaining the farm weighed heavily on everyone, especially the women. Her grandmother, Betty, worked tirelessly to provide for the family, but it was clear that farming alone was no longer enough to support them. Betty believed that education was the key to escaping the limitations of rural life, a belief that she instilled in Smarsh. Betty encouraged Smarsh to pursue education as a way to transcend the limitations that had trapped previous generations in cycles of poverty.

The farm represented both freedom and confinement for Smarsh. It was a place where hard work and resilience were celebrated, but opportunities were scarce, especially for women. As jobs dried up and families like hers were forced to leave the farm behind, many moved to cities in search of better opportunities. Smarsh reflects on how leaving the farm was both a painful loss and a necessary step for her family’s survival. While rural life had its beauty marked by a deep connection to the land and a sense of community, it often left its inhabitants feeling stuck, unable to break free without making painful sacrifices.

Despite the struggle, Smarsh found solace in the rhythms of the farm—the early mornings, the connection to the seasons, and the sense of responsibility that farm life demanded. But she also felt the pull of opportunity in the city, where education and work outside of farming seemed like the only viable path forward. Her personal escape from the confines of rural life mirrored a larger trend among working-class families who had to abandon the land that had sustained them for generations.

chapters.title: The Stigma of Poverty
chapters.text:
Psychologists explain the birth of shame as a “mechanism that evolved to control harmful behavior” to protect the community. But society shames people for being in need of economic help.

Rich whites think poor whites’ failure reflects badly on the whole race, so poor whites are made invisible. Being rendered invisible as a group is a form of invalidation, which comes with a deep shame that makes a person feel like a failure.

America’s contempt toward the poor is evident in its approach to welfare programs. Bill Clinton ushered in an era of welfare reform, where the dispersal of funds was turned over to the states’ governments. Some states chose to spend the money on workshops to promote family values that would cure poverty. In 1994, California created a costly electronic fingerprinting system for welfare recipients. The number of individuals receiving welfare assistance decreased significantly over the following twenty years.

There is a misconception in society that applying for welfare is shameful and that someone in a bad financial situation must be evil, lazy, or stupid.

To see your true potential, don’t focus on your failures.

Smarsh grew up believing she shouldn’t ask for help and developed the habit of shoplifting when she was nine. She seemed like a well-behaved, straight-A student to most people because she kept her rebellion a secret. The things she stole were mostly things she wanted but couldn’t afford.

Based on the teachings from the church and other sources, she understood that stealing was wrong. However, she saw the monetary system as unfair. She didn't feel she deserved anything but didn't expect the world to provide for her. So, she didn't take what she needed. She believed that such actions would show lousy judgment and could negatively impact her life if caught, which never happened. In fact, her school teachers saw her as smart. She even won a speech contest, making her family very proud, hugging, laughing, and taking pictures with her when she left the stage. She realized that they suffered from weakness of character just like everyone else but could love deeply in their better moments. She concluded that what really put the shame on people wasn’t their moral deficit but their money deficit.

chapters.title: Home is where the heart learns
chapters.text:
Smarsh learned renovation skills from her parents. Her dad was a carpenter, and her mom was a realtor who helped people find affordable homes and achieve homeownership. She even negotiated mortgage tricks for low-income Wichitans. They had talents when it came to houses and moved in and out of places: trailers, bad apartments, and places full of broken appliances. Sometimes, they were able to make a beautiful home out of something cheap. They valued houses for more than just the shelter they could provide but for the stability, safety, structure, and security.

Jeannie didn’t purchase more houses than she could afford; she figured life was too short and no house was truly secure. The body is the only permanent home.

Give your own definition of what home means to you.

Jeannie understood that buying a house wasn’t always easy, so she didn’t get attached to a place in case she had to move again. Her transient attitude resulted from her family, who moved from place to place in search of better lives. The constant relocations experienced by the women in her family and their daughters were a means of safeguarding themselves from abusive men and searching for alternative methods to cover their expenses.

Jeannie’s mother, Betty, left several marriages and frequently moved, which affected Jeannie in several ways—she went to four different kindergartens. Children who move around a lot lack a sense of belonging.

Smarsh eventually left home to live with her grandparents, who were different from the norm in her community, but she grew up valuing security over structures.

… no matter where you ended up, like every immigrant you’d still feel the invisible dirt of your motherland on the soles of your feet. ~ Sarah Smarsh

chapters.title: Gaining wisdom from struggles
chapters.text:
In poor and rural communities, women do not conform to gender roles because traditional expectations are not placed on them. Everyone is expected to work hard regardless of their gender. Smarsh grew up around women who ran their own families and finances.

In 1912, Kansas was the eighth state and the first in its region to grant women the right to vote in all elections. Subsequently, women also gained the right to own property and equal custody rights for their children alongside the father.

While it was a huge step toward gender equality, poor working-class women were always too busy trying to survive, so they didn't benefit from feminism.

Advocate for the people who don’t have as much privilege as others.

Women who can’t afford to leave because of domestic violence stand the chance of being killed as a result of the violence. Although Smarsh’s grandmother, Betty, was abused in her marriage, she received legal advice stating she needed a spouse to gain custody of her son. Despite leaving many bad relationships, Betty never truly became independent. She landed a position working for the state as a secretary, which changed her financial status. When she tried to get her son back again, the court sided with her ex-husband, and she never got him back. Even though she worked hard to get a steady job, she had a history with men who provided for her and her children. She had no economic power, which we also know as social power.

Smarsh believes that the experiences of the women in her family forced them into profound awareness, and she inherited old wisdom from them. They were powerful because they could bear the pain of their experiences without going numb.

Smarsh got scholarships to college — surprising everyone who knew about her upbringing—and became financially stable after leaving the farm behind. She also believed America had failed its people, but she still hoped for a better future. The greatest fortune of her life was getting out of a sense of lack and not internalizing poverty.Did you know? The Equal Rights Amendment — an amendment to the US Constitution — prohibiting discrimination based on sex, was introduced in Congress in 1923.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Poverty is more than just a lack of money — it’s a complex web of emotional, physical, and societal challenges that can trap individuals and families for generations. Smarsh’s story, rooted in the experiences of her family, shines a light on the profound, systemic nature of poverty in America. Her memoir exposes the myths surrounding the American Dream, revealing how hard work alone is rarely enough to overcome the structural barriers that keep the poor in poverty.

The women in Smarsh’s family were not just survivors; they were warriors fighting to keep their families afloat in a system stacked against them. Through their resilience, Smarsh was able to break the cycles of poverty that had held previous generations back, but her story is not just about personal success. It’s a reflection on the systemic failures that prevent so many others from achieving the same. Try this
• Delve into works like Nickel and Dimed by Barbara Ehrenreich and Evicted by Matthew Desmond to better understand the structural issues surrounding poverty.
• Engage with Communities. Volunteer with local organizations that provide support to low-income families. Community outreach offers firsthand insight and opportunities to make a difference.
• Advocate for Systemic Change. Push for policies that address wage inequality, affordable healthcare, and social safety nets to ensure poverty is treated as a systemic issue, not a personal failure.
